---
title: "The Radiology Lesson"
date: 2026-05-19
categories: [AI in Education]
description: "AI won't replace teachers. But the story that it will might keep people from becoming one."
---

I drive to Statesboro for work. It is an hour-long drive, enough to listen to podcasts but also to reflect on the changes in education, in higher ed and in K-12 (especially as a parent: "what will my son do in the future?" comes to mind a lot).

Today I was listening to Guy Raz's *How I Built This* podcast, his interview with Jensen Huang. The conversation turned to jobs and AI, close to what we have been writing about recently. Huang did not talk about teachers, but he brought up an example about radiologists that stuck with me.

He recalled how well-known experts declared that radiology would be the first profession wiped out by AI. The reasoning was straightforward: the earliest AI models were built for computer vision, computer vision could read radiology scans better than any human, and therefore no radiologists would be necessary. Huang's response: they were right. AI has transformed radiology. Every radiologist now uses AI to study scans, and it does it faster and more accurately than a human can.

But what the experts missed was the distinction between a job's **purpose** and its **tasks**. I knew about this distinction, of course, but had not thought of it in these terms. Reading scans is a lower-order task: routine, pattern-based, exactly the kind of work AI excels at offloading. But diagnosing disease is the higher-order purpose. It requires clinical judgment, context, and the ability to integrate information that no model sees. AI took over the lower-order task, and in doing so made the higher-order work more visible and more in demand. Because scanning became cheaper and faster, hospitals started ordering more scans. They saw more patients, generated more revenue, and needed to hire *more* radiologists, not fewer.

Then Huang made a point that has stayed with me. The real harm, he argued, was not the technology but the prediction itself. When well-known experts declared the end of an entire profession, young people who might have gone into radiology decided it was obsolete and chose a different path. The result: the world does not have enough radiologists. The narrative did what the technology could not.

(A grain of salt: Huang is the CEO of Nvidia. He has a vested interest in making sure people are optimistic about AI. But I think his point is worth considering.)

I believe the same dynamic is unfolding in teaching.

The dominant story right now frames GenAI as a replacement for instructional design, assessment creation, and feedback, the lower-order production tasks of teaching. And it is true that AI can handle much of this production. But production is not the purpose of teaching. The purpose is learning: designing experiences that spark curiosity, sustaining effort through relationships, mentoring the kind of thinking that no prompt can scaffold. Just as in radiology, offloading the lower-order tasks should make the higher-order work more central, not less.

If we tell the replacement story loudly enough, two things happen. Practicing teachers start ceding the higher-order work to the machine, not because the machine does it better, but because the narrative says it should. And prospective teachers never enter the profession to develop those skills in the first place.

That second risk worries me more. We are not short on AI tools for education. We are short on people who know how to teach well. Every person discouraged from entering the profession by the "AI will replace teachers" headline is a person who will never learn to design a lesson that lands, or read a room that is struggling, or figure out the right question to ask a student who has given up.

AI will change teaching. It already is. The profession will not be eliminated, but it will be reskilled around the higher-order work. The question is whether we tell that story clearly enough to keep talented people walking through the door.
